concerns.text: The central order/sheaf universality claims are broader than the proved target class. Order-equivariance is defined as ordinary finite-G equivariance; the order supplies masks and relations, and the full-OENN proof uses a global broadcast that does not depend on the order. Likewise, the sheaf specialization approximates equivariant maps on the total feature space without requiring the approximating network to commute with sheaf restrictions, while ordinary restriction-based message passing is acknowledged to be non-universal. This materially changes the interpretation of the advertised richer-order symmetry and sheaf-network UAT, although it does not invalidate the finite-group density theorem. The concern would be resolved by narrowing the claims to finite-group-equivariant maps on poset- or sheaf-indexed total spaces, or by proving density within an order-natural or restriction-compatible architecture class.

The broad CENN universal-approximation extension is not independently checkable from the submission. Essential arrow-bundle generators are deferred to prior work, the required topological and approximation hypotheses are presented only as an operational summary, and the finite OENN-realizability argument relies on unprovided details of the imported CENN construction. Because this is a headline extension beyond finite group actions, the missing definitions and proof dependencies create material uncertainty about both scope and correctness. A complete specification of the generators, hypotheses, and reduction, or explicit treatment of the general CENN theorem as imported with the contribution narrowed to the finite action-groupoid embedding, would resolve the concern.

The CENN and OENN UAT statements alternate between epsilon-density or closure language and literal equality between finite-network classes and all continuous natural or equivariant maps. The supplied argument supports density, not exact finite representability. This appears locally repairable without changing the approximation result, but the formal statements should use closure or density notation unless a separate exact-representation proof is supplied.

The claim that source labels, higher-order state, global attention, or broadcast are unavoidable is not established by the local lower bound. The theorem shows only that fewer than d_R(p,q) inter-site layers cannot transmit x_p to q; it does not prove that sufficiently deep anonymous relation-message-passing networks remain non-universal. This overstates a highlighted necessity conclusion while leaving the pair-state sufficiency construction and short-depth obstruction intact. An unrestricted-depth indistinguishability or collision theorem, or narrower wording limited to the proved depth lower bound, is needed.

The pair-state local universality result is not a bounded-width or size-uniform guarantee. Its construction stores a source-labeled copy of the total input across carriers and uses pair-dependent walk-count rescaling, so hidden-state memory and width grow with the complete input-fiber dimension. This does not undermine the theorem as stated, but it limits scalability and practical-significance interpretations. The paper should state the depth, width, and memory costs explicitly and avoid efficiency implications unless a bounded-resource result is proved.

The sole empirical study does not provide enough evidence for the claims of substantial improvement and improved generalization. The appendix omits the data split and leakage controls, metric and units, run count and uncertainty, baseline implementation and capacity controls, training details, and ablations or transformed-test checks isolating the categorical symmetry; the prose and table caption also disagree about the comparator roster. Moreover, the experiment evaluates Grothendieck CENN rather than the main OENN class. A complete numeric protocol with repeated-run uncertainty, fair tuned baselines, component and robustness ablations, and direct OENN experiments is needed to support the empirical claims.

The priority claim that no universal approximation theorem was previously known for sheaf neural networks remains unverified. The available broker-verified source establishes prior approximation theory for continuous group-equivariant maps but does not resolve overlap for poset-indexed bundles, sheaf networks, transporter parametrizations, pair-state locality, or CENNs. This is not evidence of prior overlap, but it materially limits confidence in the originality positioning. A theorem-level comparison with verified closest work, or softened priority language, is needed.
